text: The laparoscopic procedure involves the dissection and preservation of the hypogastric nerve, which is crucial for maintaining bladder, bowel, and sexual function. The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer and Bissinger bipolar for traction and counter traction, gently separating the peritoneum from the retroperitoneum in a bloodless plane. The dissection includes removal of the peritoneum and some para rectal fat to ensure a good margin without affecting bowel function.

The surgeon applies cranial traction to open and liberate the peritoneum, revealing the thickened tissue often seen in Indian women due to pelvic inflammation. The dissection progresses to strip off the entire peritoneum, leaving the rectum bare. The pararectal fossa is dissected, exposing the ureter up to the ureteric tunnel, and the uterine artery at its origin is visible, ready for coagulation.

The hypogastric nerve plexus is visible caudal to the ureter. The uterine artery at its origin is identified and coagulated using the Bissinger bipolar, with short and gentle bursts. The peritoneum is opened medial to the ureter, and dissection is performed to separate the peritoneum, highlighting the importance of traction in the procedure.

The surgeon achieves peritoneal separation and gently lifts the peritoneum away from the rectum, ensuring the entire peritoneal covering is liberated and included with the specimen. The rectum is mobilized on one side, preserving the pararectal fat and maintaining a large margin of peritoneum from the pouch of Douglas, approximately two centimeters, which is crucial for the procedure.
